title: SimPy queue simulation — comparison (multi-scenario) questions
introduction: Same model as main.py and queue_simulation.py: exponential gaps between arrivals (mean = MEAN_INTERARRIVAL minutes), exponential service (mean = MEAN_SERVICE minutes), NUM_CLERKS parallel clerks, stop at SIM_TIME minutes, RNG fixed by RANDOM_SEED. A customer is counted when they start service. Utilization = sum of sampled service lengths for those customers ÷ (NUM_CLERKS × SIM_TIME). Each lettered scenario is an independent run with its own seed and parameters. Pick the scenario that best meets the stated goal.

questions.question: You must staff a counter for 100 minutes. Arrivals average one every 3 minutes and service averages 5 minutes. Three budget options share the same randomness story except for headcount: Scenario A uses 1 clerk, B uses 2, C uses 3 (seeds 50 for each run as specified in the data). After simulating all three, which scenario minimizes the average time customers wait in line before service begins?
questions.answer: Scenario C has the lowest average queue wait.

questions.question: A rush lasts 120 minutes with tight arrivals (mean gap 2.5 minutes) and long visits (mean service 7 minutes). You can open 2, 3, or 4 windows—scenarios A, B, and C—with seeds 51, 51, 51 respectively. Which design keeps the worst customer wait (among those who eventually start service) as small as possible?
questions.answer: Scenario B minimizes the maximum queue wait.

questions.question: Over an 80-minute window, mean service is 4 minutes for all cases. Scenario A is a calm site: mean 8 minutes between arrivals and 2 clerks (seed 60). Scenario B doubles arrival intensity: mean 4 minutes between arrivals, still 2 clerks (seed 61). Scenario C matches B’s arrival rate but adds a third clerk (seed 62). Which scenario completes the largest number of customers starting service before time runs out?
questions.answer: Scenario C starts the most customers within 80 minutes.

questions.question: Traffic is light: mean 15 minutes between arrivals and mean service 5 minutes for 60 minutes on the clock. You are asked which staffing level leaves clerks idle the most (lowest utilization in the model’s sense). A runs 4 clerks (seed 70), B runs 3 (seed 71), C runs 2 (seed 72). Which scenario is best by that measure?
questions.answer: Scenario B has the lowest clerk utilization (most idle capacity).

questions.question: For 100 minutes, arrivals are aggressive (mean gap 2 minutes) and service averages 6 minutes. Compare 2, 3, and 4 clerks—scenarios A, B, and C—all using seed 85. Which scenario yields the smallest median wait among customers who begin service?
questions.answer: Scenario C achieves the lowest median queue wait (zero in this run).

questions.question: Leadership wants at least 22 customers to begin service within 100 minutes when mean interarrival time is 3 minutes and mean service is 5 minutes. Scenario A uses 2 clerks (seed 90), B uses 2 clerks with a different draw sequence (seed 91), and C uses 3 clerks (seed 92). Among only the scenarios that satisfy the 22-customer floor, which minimizes average queue wait? If several qualify, pick the one with the lowest mean wait.
questions.answer: A, B, and C all reach at least 22 starters; scenario C has the lowest average wait among them.

verification: best_scenario and metrics_by_scenario were computed with queue_simulation.run_simulation for each lettered parameter set.
